First, candidate TS structures will be located and pre-optimized in solution using density functional theory (e.g., B3LYP-D3(BJ)) with an analytical PCM gradient (state-specific formalism). These geometries will then be refined via higher-level single-point energy corrections using explicitly correlated wave-function methods (e.g., CCSD(T)-F12) on a reduced domain, with solvation effects incorporated via PCM at this final stage. The entire workflow will be automated, and key TS structures will be validated by computing anharmonic vibrational frequencies to confirm the saddle point character in solution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, practical protocol for obtaining reliable TS geometries in solution, bridging the gap between high-accuracy gas-phase quantum chemistry and realistic reaction modeling. It will provide benchmark-quality structures and solvation corrections for key organic/biochemical reaction steps, enhancing the predictive power of computational mechanistic studies.
proposals.novelty: While PCM and composite methods are established separately, their tight integration for the direct optimization of transition states—rather than single-point corrections—is novel. This moves beyond the standard practice of post-optimization solvation, directly addressing the geometry relaxation induced by the solvent during the TS search.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Hybrid DFT Functionals for Large Flexible Systems: A Systematic Assessment of mPWPW and mPW1PW on Supramolecular Complexes and Biomolecular Conformers
proposals.motivation: The accurate computational determination of structures and spectroscopic parameters for large, flexible biomolecules and supramolecular complexes remains a significant challenge in quantum chemistry. While composite wave-function methods offer high accuracy, they are prohibitively expensive for systems beyond ~50 atoms. The widely used, more feasible hybrid DFT functionals, such as the mPWPW and mPW1PW families, have been parameterized and validated primarily on small molecules, leaving their performance for large, conformationally rich systems largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mPWPW and mPW1PW functionals will demonstrate a systematic, size-dependent deviation in accuracy for large molecular complexes, particularly for non-covalent interaction energies and relative conformational energies, which are critical for predicting reliable rotational constants and spectroscopic signatures.
proposals.method: We will perform an extensive benchmark on a curated set of 15-20 systems, including host-guest complexes (e.g., cyclodextrin adducts), small protein fragments, and multi-conformer biomolecules (e.g., dipeptides). For each system, we will compute optimized geometries, conformational energy rankings, and rotational constants using mPWPW, mPW1PW, and a range of basis sets. These results will be rigorously compared against a gold-standard reference set generated via a composite wave-function strategy (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS on DFT-optimized geometries) for smaller fragments and against high-resolution experimental microwave/rotational spectroscopy data from the literature where available.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, quantitative accuracy assessment of these popular hybrid DFT functionals for large, relevant chemical systems. It will deliver clear, practical guidelines for computational spectroscopists and biochemists on the expected accuracy and limitations of these methods when applied to supramolecular and biomolecular modeling, directly informing future studies on molecular bricks of life.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the benchmarking paradigm from small, rigid molecules to the large, flexible complexes where these methods are actually applied in cutting-edge research, and in directly linking functional performance to the accuracy of predicted spectroscopic observables like rotational constants.

proposals.title: Environment-Dependent Atomic Radii for Accurate Conformer-Specific Molecular Properties: A Machine-Learning Enhanced Quantum Chemical Strategy
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of spectroscopic observables, such as rotational constants, for flexible biomolecules requires precise equilibrium structures. Current computational protocols often use fixed atomic radii for geometry corrections or continuum solvation models, neglecting that an atom's effective size depends on its local conformation and chemical environment (e.g., hybridization, hydrogen bonding). This introduces systematic errors in computed properties across a conformational landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deriving conformationally dependent atomic radii from high-level ab initio electron density will yield more accurate and transferable corrections for molecular geometries, leading to improved agreement between computed and experimental spectroscopic parameters for each individual conformer.
proposals.method: We will generate a benchmark dataset of accurate structures and electron densities for diverse conformers of amino acids (e.g., phenylalanine, 4-fluoro-threonine) using our established composite wave-function (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS) and DFT strategies. A machine learning model (specifically a graph neural network) will be trained to predict atomic radii as a function of local descriptors: bond orders, atomic partial charges (from NBO), ring critical point densities, and dihedral angles. These dynamic radii will be integrated into geometry refinement protocols and continuum solvation calculations to assess their impact on final rotational constants and vibrational frequencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physically grounded method for conformation-specific atomic radii, directly enhancing the accuracy of structure-based spectroscopic predictions. It will provide a new tool for the detailed interpretation of microwave and rotational spectroscopy data, crucial for the study of complex biomolecular building blocks in the gas phase.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been used for property prediction, its application to derive geometry-dependent, fundamental atomic parameters from first-principles electron density for spectroscopic accuracy is unexplored. This moves beyond static tabulated radii to a dynamic, quantum chemically informed model.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Implicit/Explicit Solvation Strategy for Accurate Conformer-Specific Spectroscopic Parameters of Biomolecules
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of spectroscopic parameters for biomolecules in solution is crucial for interpreting experiments but remains challenging. Pure implicit solvation models (e.g., PCM) often fail to capture specific, directional solute-solvent interactions like hydrogen bonds, while fully explicit solvent simulations are computationally prohibitive for high-level quantum chemical structure refinement. A robust, accurate, and feasible middle ground is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid solvation model, combining a continuum dielectric cavity with a single, explicitly modeled shell of solvent molecules at the molecular surface, will yield conformational structures, rotational constants, and vibrational frequencies for solvated biomolecules with accuracy approaching that of high-level gas-phase calculations.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical computational strategy. First, conformational landscapes of target molecules (e.g., amino acids like serine or small peptides) will be explored using DFT-D3 with an implicit solvent model. For key low-energy conformers, we will construct a first-solvation-shell complex by placing explicit water molecules at probable hydrogen-bonding sites. The geometry of this solute-(explicit shell) cluster will then be optimized using a cost-effective yet accurate composite wave-function method (e.g., r^2SCAN-3c or a double-hybrid DFT). Finally, this optimized cluster will be embedded in a continuum model (SMD) to account for bulk solvent effects, and spectroscopic parameters will be computed at this hybrid level.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, practical computational protocol for predicting solvent-influenced spectroscopic properties with benchmark accuracy. It will provide validated structures and spectroscopic signatures for key biomolecular building blocks in aqueous solution, directly aiding the interpretation of jet-cooled and liquid-phase spectroscopic experiments. The protocol will bridge the gap between high-accuracy gas-phase studies and realistic solvated environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of a *defined* explicit solvent shell into Barone's established composite-method workflow for spectroscopic accuracy, moving beyond parameterized continuum models or stochastic explicit sampling to a deterministic, quantum-mechanically refined treatment of the critical first solvation sphere.

proposals.title: Benchmarking a Machine-Learning Derived Cavity Definition for Accurate Conformer Structures: A High-Level CCSD(T) and Composite Method Validation
proposals.motivation: Recent work has introduced a machine-learning-enhanced strategy to define effective cavity models for predicting rotational constants of flexible biomolecules, improving agreement with microwave spectroscopy. However, the initial validation relied heavily on DFT and moderate-level wave-function methods. To establish this as a robust, general-purpose tool for conformational landscape exploration, it is critical to test its performance against the highest-accuracy quantum chemical benchmarks available pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the new, data-driven cavity definition will maintain or improve its predictive accuracy for molecular structures and rotational constants when the reference data is generated using high-level post-Hartree-Fock methods and large basis sets, confirming its transferability beyond the DFT level.
proposals.method: We will select a benchmark set of flexible amino acids (e.g., phenylalanine, tyrosine, 4-fluoro-threonine) and their low-energy conformers. For each, we will compute highly accurate reference geometries and rotational constants using a composite wave-function strategy: geometries will be optimized at the CCSD(T)/cc-pVTZ level where feasible, or using a CBS+CV+rel composite scheme. Single-point energy refinements will be performed with even larger basis sets (e.g., cc-pVQZ, aug-cc-pVQZ). These benchmark values will be compared against predictions from our established protocol, where the DFT-optimized structures are corrected using the new machine-learning-derived cavity model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a stringent, high-level quantum chemical validation of a promising new method for spectroscopic accuracy, solidifying its reliability for the molecular spectroscopy community. It will also deliver a new set of CCSD(T)-level reference structures and spectroscopic parameters for key biological building blocks, a valuable resource for future method development.
proposals.novelty: While composite wave-function methods are known for accuracy, their systematic application to validate a machine-learning-enhanced empirical correction for conformational spectroscopy is novel. This bridges the gap between high-accuracy quantum chemistry and practical, feasible spectroscopic prediction for large, flexible systems.

proposals.title: Benchmarking a Hybrid DFT/Composite Wave-Function Strategy with Implicit Solvation for the Conformational Landscapes of Neurotransmitter Precursors
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of molecular structures and spectroscopic parameters for flexible, biologically relevant molecules in solution remains a significant challenge in computational chemistry. Pre-2026 work has validated hybrid DFT/composite wave-function methods and implicit solvation models on small, rigid systems, but their transferability to larger, multi-functional molecules with complex conformational landscapes is untested. This gap limits the reliable computational study of key biomolecular building blocks under physiologically relevant conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The joint DFT/composite wave-function strategy, coupled with a robust implicit solvation model (e.g., SMD, PCM), can accurately predict the relative stability, structures, and rotational constants of low-energy conformers for complex, flexible biomolecules like L-DOPA and serotonin precursors, achieving spectroscopic accuracy suitable for guiding and interpreting gas-phase microwave experiments.
proposals.method: We will select target molecules (e.g., 5-HTP, tyrosine derivatives) known for rich conformational landscapes and biological relevance. A systematic conformational search will be performed using molecular mechanics and DFT (B3LYP-D3(BJ)/def2-TZVP) with an implicit solvation model (SMD for water). Low-energy conformers will then be refined using a composite wave-function method (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS-type extrapolation or r^2SCAN-3c) on the DFT-optimized geometries, with single-point energy corrections incorporating solvation effects. Final predicted rotational constants and relative Gibbs free energies will be compared against available or newly acquired experimental microwave data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical validation of a high-accuracy computational protocol for complex, solvated systems, directly extending the 'joint venture' philosophy to pharmacologically relevant molecules. It will deliver benchmark-quality structures and spectroscopic parameters for neurotransmitter precursors, establishing a reliable path for studying biologically active conformers and informing future laser ablation or jet-cooled spectroscopic studies.
proposals.novelty: While the hybrid method has been proven for gas-phase amino acids, its rigorous integration with implicit solvation to resolve the conformational preferences of larger, polyfunctional biomolecules—where solvent interactions are crucial—is a novel and necessary step toward predictive computational biochemistry.

proposals.title: Solvent Effects on Electronic Spectral Intensities: A Hybrid Quantum-Mechanical/Machine-Learning Protocol for Accurate Oscillator Strengths of Bio-Organic Molecules
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 computational spectroscopy has focused heavily on predicting solvent-induced shifts in transition energies (e.g., absorption/emission maxima), while solvent effects on spectral intensities, governed by oscillator strengths, remain less explored and often less accurate. For key bio-organic molecules like amino acids and neurotransmitters, accurate prediction of absorption band intensities in aqueous or lipid environments is crucial for interpreting experimental UV-Vis spectra and designing optical probes, but current implicit solvation models struggle with the specific solute-solvent interactions that modulate transition dipole moments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strategy combining high-level composite wave-function methods for the solute's electronic structure with a machine-learning (ML) model trained on specific solute-solvent interaction energies can accurately predict the solvent-induced changes in oscillator strengths, going beyond the limitations of continuum solvation models.
proposals.method: We will extend our established joint DFT/composite wave-function (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS) protocol for gas-phase structures and properties. For target molecules (e.g., tyrosine, tryptophan derivatives), we will generate a diverse conformational ensemble in solution using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) with an explicit solvent shell. An ML model (e.g., a kernel-based or neural network potential) will be trained on interaction energies between the solute's key fragments and explicit solvent molecules from short AIMD trajectories. This ML-augmented model will then be used to generate statistically representative solvated structures for which oscillator strengths will be computed using time-dependent DFT with a high-accuracy functional, explicitly incorporating the electrostatic and polarization effects of the first solvation shell, while a continuum model handles bulk effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first systematically accurate protocol for predicting solvent shifts on oscillator strengths for flexible bio-organic molecules, directly aiding the interpretation of experimental solution-phase electronic spectra. It will extend the 'joint venture' philosophy from structures and rotational constants to a critical, underexplored spectral parameter, enhancing the predictive power of computational chemistry for real-world spectroscopic applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of an ML-enhanced quantum chemistry strategy to predict solvent effects on transition intensities (oscillator strengths), rather than just energies, moving beyond the state-of-the-art focus on band positions to fully characterize spectral line shapes in solution.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Solvation Strategy for Accurate Excited-State Properties of Biomolecular Building Blocks: Merging TD-DFT/PCM with an Explicit MM Solvent Shell
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of electronic excitations in biomolecules like amino acids requires a realistic description of solvation. Pure implicit solvation models (e.g., PCM) often fail to capture specific, directional solute-solvent interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonds), while full quantum-mechanical treatment of explicit solvent is computationally prohibitive for spectroscopic accuracy. A robust hybrid approach is needed to bridge this gap for reliable predictions of UV-Vis spectra and excited-state dynamics in solution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a TD-DFT/PCM-treated solute within a molecular mechanics (MM) solvent shell will yield more accurate excited-state structures, energies, and spectroscopic parameters than pure PCM, by explicitly modeling short-range electrostatic and polarization effects while maintaining computational feasibility for conformational exploration.
proposals.method: We will employ a QM/MM scheme where the core solute is treated with TD-DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-TZVP) coupled to a Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM). This QM/PCM core will be embedded in an explicit shell of solvent molecules (e.g., water) described by a polarizable force field (e.g., AMOEBA). The geometry of the solute and the first solvation shell will be optimized using a mechanical embedding scheme. Spectroscopic parameters (vertical and adiabatic excitation energies, oscillator strengths) will be computed for key conformers of phenylalanine and tyrosine, benchmarking against available gas-phase data and solution-phase experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated and practical computational protocol for simulating the electronic spectroscopy of flexible biomolecules in solution with unprecedented accuracy. It will provide fundamental insights into how explicit solvent structuring modulates the excited-state properties of life's molecular bricks, directly aiding the interpretation of experimental spectra.
proposals.novelty: While QM/MM is established for ground states, its systematic application to TD-DFT for *conformationally flexible* biomolecules, synergistically combined with an implicit model to capture long-range bulk effects, represents a novel strategy for spectroscopic accuracy in solution, extending Barone's established 'joint venture' philosophy to excited-state solvation.

proposals.title: Accurate UV-Vis Spectra of Nucleobases and Neurotransmitters in Aqueous Solution: A SS-PCM-TD-DFT Benchmark and Application Study
proposals.motivation: The spectroscopic properties of biological chromophores, such as nucleobases and neurotransmitters, are fundamentally altered by their aqueous environment, impacting processes like photosynthesis, DNA damage, and signaling. Pre-2026 computational studies often treat these large molecules in isolation or use implicit solvation models not explicitly validated for excited-state properties, creating a gap between theoretical predictions and experimental solution-phase spectra.
proposals.hypothesis: The state-specific polarizable continuum model (SS-PCM) for time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) can provide quantitatively accurate vertical excitation energies and spectral shapes for large biological molecules in water when benchmarked against high-level wave-function references and experimental data for key chromophores.
proposals.method: We will apply the SS-PCM-TD-DFT methodology, using range-separated hybrid functionals (e.g., ωB97X-D), to compute the UV-Vis spectra of prototypical biological chromophores (e.g., adenine, serotonin) in water. The strategy involves: 1) a conformational search using cost-effective DFT-D methods; 2) a rigorous benchmark for smaller model systems where SS-PCM-TD-DFT results are compared to extrapolated CC3/PCM or CASPT2/PCM reference data; 3) application to the larger target molecules, with spectra constructed using a Boltzmann-weighted average over low-energy conformers identified via machine-learning-accelerated sampling, continuing the group's established joint venture approach.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated computational protocol for predicting solution-phase electronic spectra of biomolecules, bridging the gap between high-accuracy gas-phase studies and biological reality. It will provide new, accurate reference data for key chromophores and establish the practical limits of SS-PCM-TD-DFT for systems of direct biochemical interest.
proposals.novelty: While SS-PCM is known, its systematic application and validation for the excited states of large, flexible biological molecules—integrating conformational averaging, benchmark-quality references, and the group's expertise in composite methods—is a significant and non-routine advancement beyond standard TD-DFT/PCM calculations.

proposals.title: Broadening the Scope of Solvated Excited-State Calculations: Implementation and Benchmarking of the State-Specific PCM-TD-DFT Method in ORCA and PySCF
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of electronic excitations in solution is crucial for interpreting UV-Vis spectra and understanding photophysics in biological and materials chemistry. The state-specific polarizable continuum model (SS-PCM) for time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) offers improved accuracy over standard linear-response approaches by allowing solvent polarization to equilibrate with each specific excited state, but its availability is limited to a few codes. This restricts widespread adoption and cross-validation, hindering its application to the large, flexible molecules of spectroscopic interest.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a robust, efficient SS-PCM-TD-DFT module in two widely-used, open-source quantum chemistry packages (ORCA and PySCF) will enable more accurate and accessible calculations of solvated excited-state structures and properties for complex molecules, directly benefiting spectroscopic accuracy.
proposals.method: We will implement the SS-PCM-TD-DFT formalism, following the established work of Improta and Barone, into the ORCA and PySCF software packages. The implementation will involve coding the state-specific solvation correction into the TD-DFT gradient and property routines, utilizing each code's existing PCM and TD-DFT infrastructure. We will validate the implementation by benchmarking against published results for small organic chromophores and then apply it to calculate the solvent-dependent vertical and adiabatic excitation energies, and subsequently the gas-phase-to-solution shifts in spectroscopic parameters, for key conformers of amino acid side-chain analogs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the computational chemistry community with two new, freely available, and well-documented tools for high-accuracy solvated excited-state calculations. It will extend the proven 'joint venture' strategy of combining DFT and wave-function methods to the excited-state realm in solution, enabling more reliable interpretations of experimental spectra for biologically relevant molecules.
proposals.novelty: While the SS-PCM-TD-DFT theory is established, its integration into major, community-driven open-source platforms like PySCF and its extension within the highly efficient ORCA code is novel. This moves beyond single-code implementations, creating a portable benchmark standard and enabling large-scale conformational sampling of excited states in solution, which aligns with but significantly expands the team's recent machine-learning enhanced conformational exploration work.

proposals.title: Decoding the First Solvation Shell: Accurate Structures and Spectroscopic Fingerprints of Micro-Solvated Amino Acids via a Hybrid Quantum Chemistry and Machine Learning Strategy
proposals.motivation: Understanding the fundamental interactions between a solute and its first few solvent molecules is crucial for bridging isolated gas-phase studies and bulk solvation effects. Pre-2026, the conformational landscapes and spectroscopic signatures of micro-solvated clusters of biologically relevant molecules remain poorly characterized with high accuracy, limiting our atomistic understanding of solvation onset.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of a composite quantum chemistry strategy, enhanced by machine learning for conformational sampling, to micro-solvated clusters of amino acids will yield benchmark structures and rotational constants, revealing how individual solvent molecules selectively stabilize specific solute conformers and perturb its spectroscopic properties.
proposals.method: We will apply the established 'cheap-expensive' strategy: (1) Use DFT (e.g., r2SCAN-3c) with a genetic algorithm and a machine-learned potential (e.g., ANI-type or kernel ridge regression) to exhaustively sample conformers of clusters like phenylalanine-(H2O)n (n=1-3). (2) Refine low-energy candidates using a composite wave-function method (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS-type corrections on MP2 geometries) to achieve spectroscopic accuracy. (3) Calculate anharmonic vibrational and precise rotational constants for direct comparison with potential future jet-cooled microwave or IR spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first highly accurate structural and spectroscopic database for key micro-solvated amino acid clusters, quantifying the incremental effect of individual solvent molecules. It will establish a rigorous protocol for studying solvation at its most fundamental level, directly linking quantum chemistry predictions to experimental observables for clusters.
proposals.novelty: While the researcher's group has studied isolated amino acids and used ML for conformational search, the systematic application of their high-accuracy composite protocol to explicitly solvated clusters, targeting the precise spectroscopy of solute-solvent molecular units, is a novel and logical scaling-down of the 'Spectroscopic Accuracy' (SS) concept.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Molecular Conformational Energies and Spectroscopic Properties with Solid-State Validation Protocols: A New Standard for Accuracy
proposals.motivation: In quantum chemistry, benchmark datasets for molecular properties like conformational energies and rotational constants are often limited in size and diversity, potentially biasing method validation. Conversely, the solid-state community has developed extensive, high-throughput benchmark protocols for properties like band gaps, using large, curated materials databases. Applying these rigorous, large-scale validation frameworks to molecular problems could reveal systematic errors in computational strategies that smaller benchmarks miss.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying high-throughput, solid-state-inspired benchmarking protocols to molecular conformational landscapes will expose systematic inaccuracies in widely-used DFT and composite wave-function methods for key spectroscopic properties, leading to more robust and transferable computational strategies.
proposals.method: We will adapt high-throughput computational workflows from materials science (e.g., using AFLOW or Materials Project-like automation) to generate a large, diverse benchmark set of molecular conformers, focusing on biologically relevant building blocks like amino acids. For each system, we will compute conformational energy hierarchies, rotational constants, and vibrational frequencies using a hierarchy of methods (DFT functionals, double-hybrids, and composite wave-function approaches like CCSD(T)-F12). The key innovation is the systematic, large-scale statistical analysis of errors, mirroring how band gaps are validated across hundreds of solids, to identify method performance trends and outliers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, more statistically rigorous benchmark standard for molecular quantum chemistry. It will provide clear guidelines for selecting computational methods for accurate spectroscopic prediction, directly benefiting fields like astrochemistry and molecular physics. The resulting public database of computed properties will serve as a critical testbed for future method development, including machine-learning force fields.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist, the direct translation of the scale, automation, and statistical rigor of solid-state materials validation to the problem of molecular conformers and spectroscopy is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond testing on a few known systems to a high-throughput, discovery-oriented validation paradigm.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic-Driven Strategy for Excitation-Specific Quantum Chemical Methods: Breaking the Uniform Performance Paradigm for Accurate Vibrational Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Current strategies for predicting vibrational spectra, especially for flexible biomolecules, often rely on applying a single, high-level composite method uniformly across all normal modes. This is computationally prohibitive and unnecessary, as different types of molecular motions (e.g., soft torsions vs. stiff bond stretches) have varying sensitivities to electron correlation and basis set effects. A diagnostic to select the optimal, cost-effective method for each excitation type would dramatically enhance the feasibility of achieving spectroscopic accuracy for complex systems, a core challenge in pre-2026 computational spectroscopy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simple, pre-computable molecular descriptors (e.g., mode-specific electron density descriptors, localized orbital energies, or Hessian matrix eigenvalues) can reliably predict the required level of theory for each vibrational mode, enabling a targeted, multi-method computational strategy that matches or exceeds the accuracy of a uniform high-level approach at a fraction of the cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a machine-learning diagnostic by first generating a benchmark dataset of accurate anharmonic frequencies for key model systems (amino acids, nucleic acid bases) using high-level CCSD(T)-based composite approaches. For each normal mode, we will compute a vector of low-cost descriptors from DFT (e.g., B3LYP-D3) calculations. A supervised learning model (e.g., a Random Forest or Gradient Boosting regressor) will be trained to predict the error of lower-cost methods (e.g., DFT functionals, MP2) relative to the benchmark for each mode type. The final diagnostic will classify modes into categories requiring specific, optimal method tiers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, generalizable protocol for 'smart' computational spectroscopy, moving beyond one-method-fits-all approaches. It will directly enable the accurate and feasible simulation of vibrational spectra for larger biomolecules by intelligently allocating computational resources. The resulting diagnostic tool will be a significant advance for the field of computational chemistry, bridging high-accuracy wavefunction methods and efficient DFT in a principled, automated manner.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning is used to predict spectra or energies, its application as a *prescriptive* diagnostic to select the quantum chemical method *per vibrational excitation* based on local molecular properties is a novel paradigm. This challenges the entrenched assumption that uniform method application is the only path to accuracy.

proposals.title: Decoding Deceptive Simplicity: A Quantum-Chemical and Spectroscopic Investigation of Molecules with Simple Electronic Structure but Complex Conformational-Rotational Fingerprints
proposals.motivation: Current computational spectroscopy benchmarks often focus on molecules with inherently complex electronic structure, where high-level methods are obviously required. However, a significant challenge for automated spectral assignment and force field development lies in molecules that appear electronically simple (e.g., single-reference, small dipole) yet yield deceptively dense or complex rotational spectra due to shallow potential energy surfaces, numerous low-energy conformers, and intricate coupling effects. Identifying and understanding these 'stealth' cases is crucial for improving the robustness of quantum chemistry workflows in spectroscopy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct class of molecules exists where standard DFT methods (e.g., B3LYP, ωB97X-D) predict a simple electronic and conformational landscape, but more accurate composite wave-function methods (e.g., CCSD(T)-F12/cc-pVTZ-F12 on DFT geometries) reveal a dense manifold of near-degenerate conformers whose predicted rotational spectra are complex and challenging to assign, despite the simple underlying electronic structure.
proposals.method: We will invert the typical selection logic by first using a low-cost DFT (B3LYP-D3/ma-def2-TZVP) pre-screening to identify candidate molecules (e.g., medium-sized flexible organics with O, N, F substituents) that appear to have a single dominant conformer or a few well-separated ones. For these candidates, we will then apply our established 'cheap-but-accurate' composite wave-function strategy (e.g., r^2SCAN-3c geometries with MP2/CBS+CV harmonic frequencies and CCSD(T)-F12/cc-pVTZ-F12 single-point refinements) to map the true conformational landscape. Machine learning (specifically, a conformer-specific graph neural network regressor) will be trained on the discrepancies between DFT and composite method relative energies to predict new deceptive candidates. Predicted rotational constants and spectra for all low-energy conformers will be computed and compared for complexity metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a curated benchmark set of molecules that are critical stress-tests for computational spectroscopy protocols, highlighting the limitations of DFT for conformational populations in seemingly simple cases. It will deliver highly accurate spectroscopic parameters for these challenging systems, aiding their experimental detection. Furthermore, the developed ML model will serve as a warning system to flag potentially deceptive molecules before costly experimental or high-level computational investment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically seeking out and characterizing molecules that are problematic precisely because they are *not* obvious targets for high-level theory, thereby inverting the standard benchmarking paradigm. It combines Barone's expertise in composite methods with a proactive ML-driven search for failure modes, rather than successes, in computational spectroscopy.

proposals.title: Beyond Vertical Excitations: A Hybrid Quantum Chemistry Strategy for Mapping Excited-State Potential Energy Surfaces of Bio-Relevant Molecules
proposals.motivation: Current computational studies of electronically excited states, particularly for benchmarking, predominantly focus on vertical excitation energies at ground-state geometries. However, photochemical processes and non-radiative decay pathways are governed by the topology of the full excited-state potential energy surface (PES), including conical intersections and minima. For biologically relevant molecules like amino acids, a lack of accurate, computationally feasible PES mapping limits our understanding of their photophysics and photostability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a judicious, multi-level computational strategy, combining machine-learned potentials, density functional theory (DFT), and high-level wave-function methods, can deliver accurate excited-state PES maps for medium-sized biomolecules at a feasible computational cost, revealing critical non-adiabatic pathways missed by vertical excitation studies.
proposals.method: We will extend our established 'joint venture' strategy to excited states. For target molecules (e.g., tyrosine, tryptophan), we will first perform extensive conformer exploration in the ground state using DFT-D3. Key regions of the excited-state PES (S1, T1) will then be sampled via time-dependent DFT (TD-ωB97X-D) and CASSCF calculations. These high-level single-point energies will train a machine-learning potential (using a kernel-based or neural network approach like SchNet) to interpolate and construct a continuous, accurate PES. Critical points (minima, conical intersections) will be refined using composite wave-function methods (e.g., NEVPT2/CASSCF).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first accurate, comprehensive excited-state PES maps for fundamental biochromophores, directly linking their structure to photodynamic behavior. It will establish a robust and transferable protocol for moving beyond vertical benchmarks to dynamics-relevant spectroscopy, offering predictive insight into photodegradation and energy dissipation mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of ML-aided PES construction with composite quantum chemistry, specifically tailored for the excited-state conformational landscapes of flexible biomolecules—a significant shift from the standard practice of calculating only vertical excitations for benchmarking.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Multireference, Coupled-Cluster, and TD-DFT Approaches for Electronic Excitations in Larger Biomolecular Chromophores: The Case of Tryptophan and Tyrosine-Containing Dipeptides
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of electronic excitations in biomolecules is crucial for interpreting UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, yet the performance of quantum chemical methods for larger, flexible systems with diverse excitations (e.g., ππ*, nπ*, charge-transfer) remains poorly benchmarked. Pre-2026 studies on single amino acids lack the complexity of peptide environments, where conformational flexibility and intramolecular interactions can significantly alter excited-state landscapes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for larger, conformationally flexible biomolecular chromophores, the accuracy hierarchy of high-level wavefunction methods (e.g., CASPT2, NEVPT2, CCSD) versus more feasible TD-DFT will be excitation-specific and strongly dependent on the chosen conformer, revealing critical thresholds where single-reference methods become inadequate.
proposals.method: We will apply the established joint strategy from Barone's recent work: 1) Conformational sampling via DFT-D3/MM molecular dynamics and geometry optimization. 2) For key conformers, compute vertical excitation energies using a multi-tier approach: TD-DFT with a range of functionals (e.g., ωB97X-D, CAM-B3LYP), single-reference CC methods (CC2, CCSD), and multireference methods (CASSCF/NEVPT2) for suspect states. 3) Benchmark against available gas-phase experimental data or higher-level EOM-CCSD(T) benchmarks for smaller fragments. The study will focus on tryptophan and tyrosine-containing dipeptides as paradigmatic systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, systematic accuracy assessment for excited-state methods applied to biologically relevant chromophores beyond monomeric units. It will deliver a validated, cost-accuracy balanced protocol for predicting spectra of flexible biomolecules and identify specific electronic signatures that necessitate multireference treatment.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist for small molecules and single amino acids, a systematic, conformer-specific comparison targeting diverse excitation types in peptide-linked chromophores—directly bridging spectroscopic accuracy and biomolecular complexity—is novel and addresses a clear gap in pre-2026 computational spectroscopy.

proposals.title: Accurate Modeling of Nicotine in a Protein Binding Pocket: A Hybrid QM/MM and Machine-Learning Strategy for Biochemical Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Understanding the specific interactions of small molecules like nicotine with protein targets, such as nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, is crucial for biochemistry and drug design. Pre-2026, high-accuracy spectroscopic predictions for flexible biomolecules were largely confined to the gas phase, creating a significant gap to the realistic, solvated protein environment where function occurs. Bridging this gap by scaling up accurate quantum chemical methods to model ligand-protein complexes is a critical next step for predictive biochemical spectroscopy.
proposals.hypothesis: The application of a hierarchical computational strategy, combining machine-learned potentials, density functional theory (DFT), and composite wave-function methods within a quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) framework, can predict the structure, conformational landscape, and spectroscopic signatures of nicotine in a protein binding pocket with accuracy approaching that of gas-phase benchmark studies.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step protocol. First, the conformational landscape of nicotine will be explored using machine-learning accelerated molecular dynamics (ML-MD) with semi-empirical or DFTB potentials. Key conformers will be embedded in a representative protein binding pocket (e.g., from the acetylcholine-binding protein) using a QM/MM setup. High-level single-point energy corrections on the QM region will be computed using our established composite wave-function schemes (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS-type corrections on DFT geometries). Finally, this hybrid data will be used to refine a specific, system-tailored ML potential to efficiently compute accurate rotational constants and vibrational frequencies for the bound ligand.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first highly accurate, computationally feasible model for the spectroscopic parameters of a pharmacologically relevant molecule within a realistic protein environment. It will establish a generalizable protocol for scaling 'gold-standard' quantum chemistry to biomolecular complexes, directly aiding the interpretation of future experimental spectra of protein-ligand systems and providing unprecedented insight into interaction-induced structural distortions.
proposals.novelty: While QM/MM is standard, the novelty lies in integrating Barone's proven hierarchical quantum chemistry strategy—developed for gas-phase accuracy—with modern ML-acceleration specifically to achieve spectroscopic accuracy for a flexible ligand within a dynamic protein pocket, a significantly more complex challenge than gas-phase or implicit solvation studies.

proposals.title: Inverse Spectroscopic Design of Bio-Relevant Molecules via a Hybrid Quantum-Chemical and Machine Learning Strategy
proposals.motivation: The forward prediction of spectroscopic properties from molecular structure is a cornerstone of computational chemistry, enabling the interpretation of experimental spectra. However, the inverse problem—designing molecules to match a target excitation spectrum—remains a significant challenge, particularly for flexible, biologically relevant systems where conformational landscapes critically influence spectral signatures. Solving this inverse design problem would accelerate the discovery of molecular probes, biomarkers, and systems with tailored photophysical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating high-accuracy quantum-chemical methods for spectroscopic parameter prediction with generative machine learning models, it is possible to efficiently invert the structure-spectrum relationship and propose candidate molecular structures for a target rotational-vibrational excitation spectrum.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, closed-loop framework. First, a conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) or a graph-based generative model will be trained on a diverse dataset of molecular structures and their corresponding accurate spectroscopic parameters (rotational constants, anharmonic frequencies) computed using our established composite wave-function/DFT strategy. Second, an iterative refinement loop will be implemented, where the generative model proposes candidate structures, their spectra are computed via the accurate quantum-chemical protocol, and the discrepancy from the target spectrum is used to retrain or guide the generative model via reinforcement learning or Bayesian optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational protocol for the *de novo* design of molecules with desired spectroscopic fingerprints, moving beyond mere spectral assignment. It will provide a concrete, validated methodology for the inverse design problem in spectroscopy, applied to the complex conformational landscapes of amino acid derivatives or similar 'molecular bricks of life'. The resulting open-source tool will bridge quantum chemistry and generative AI for molecular discovery.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been used for forward property prediction and generative chemistry for drug-like properties, its application to the precise inverse design of molecules based on high-accuracy *spectroscopic* targets—requiring the prediction of subtle conformational effects—is a novel and non-trivial integration of fields. This directly inverts the traditional workflow of spectroscopic analysis.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Multireference/PCM Strategy for Solvent-Modulated Doubly Excited States: Benchmarking on Biochromophores
proposals.motivation: Accurately modeling electronically excited states, particularly challenging doubly excited states, is crucial for understanding photophysics in complex molecules like biochromophores. Standard Time-Dependent Density Functional Theory (TDDFT) often fails for these multiconfigurational states, while accurate multireference wavefunction methods are computationally prohibitive, especially when solvent effects via continuum models like the Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM) are required. A practical, accurate, and solvent-aware method for these excitations is a significant pre-2026 gap.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid strategy, which uses multireference perturbation theory (e.g., CASPT2 or NEVPT2) to correct single-reference TDDFT energies for doubly excited character, can be effectively integrated with a state-specific PCM framework to deliver accurate solvent-shifted excitation energies for biologically relevant conformers.
proposals.method: The project will develop and implement a computational protocol: (1) Identify candidate states using TDDFT and wavefunction diagnostics (e.g., T1, D1). (2) For states with strong multireference character, compute high-level gas-phase corrections using a composite wavefunction strategy (e.g., CCSD(T) or CASPT2 on TDDFT-optimized geometries). (3) Integrate these corrections with a state-specific, non-equilibrium PCM treatment within the TDDFT framework to account for solvent polarization. (4) Validate the protocol on paradigmatic systems (e.g., nucleobases, aromatic amino acids) against available high-level benchmarks and, where possible, experimental gas-phase and solution spectroscopic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical computational strategy for predicting solvent effects on difficult excitations beyond standard TDDFT, directly applicable to the spectroscopic analysis of biomolecular building blocks. It will deliver benchmark-quality excitation energies and solvent shifts for key biochromophores, enhancing the interpretation of UV-Vis spectra in realistic environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a multireference correction scheme with a state-specific solvation model for doubly excited states, moving beyond gas-phase benchmarks to create a tool for predicting solvent-modulated photophysics where conventional methods are known to fail.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Solvated Excited-State Geometries: A Systematic Validation of PCM-TDDFT Against High-Level Wavefunction Methods for Bio-Organic Chromophores
proposals.motivation: Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM)-TDDFT is the workhorse for modeling excited states in solution, crucial for interpreting UV-Vis spectra and photochemistry of biomolecules. However, its accuracy for predicting excited-state geometries and vibrational properties in a solvent environment is not systematically established against reliable benchmarks, creating uncertainty in computational spectroscopy. A rigorous validation using high-level ab initio methods is needed to define the limits and optimal strategies for PCM-TDDFT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite wavefunction strategy, combining accurate solvated ground-state references with high-level excited-state calculations (e.g., CC2, CASPT2), can provide reliable benchmarks to quantify the performance of PCM-TDDFT functionals for excited-state structures and derived spectroscopic parameters.
proposals.method: We will select a set of 10-15 bio-organic chromophores (e.g., from phenylalanine, tyrosine analogs) with known conformational sensitivity. For each, we will compute ground-state equilibrium geometries in solution using a composite wavefunction approach (e.g., CCSD(T)-F12/cc-pVTZ-F12 with PCM). Excited-state geometries will then be optimized using both PCM-TDDFT (with a range of functionals) and high-level wavefunction methods (e.g., PCM-CC2, or SS-CASPT2 with implicit solvation). Key properties—vertical and adiabatic excitation energies, excited-state rotational constants, and harmonic vibrational frequencies—will be compared statistically.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark dataset of solvated excited-state geometries from high-level theory for biologically relevant molecules. It will yield clear, actionable guidelines for selecting PCM-TDDFT functionals and protocols to achieve spectroscopic accuracy, directly benefiting computational photochemistry and spectroscopy. The results will extend the group's established 'joint venture' strategy from ground-state to excited-state conformational landscapes.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist for gas-phase excited states, a systematic assessment focusing on *solvated geometries* and *derived rotational/vibrational parameters* against high-level wavefunction methods is novel. The integration of composite-method accuracy with explicit solvation for excited-state structural validation represents a significant advance beyond standard TDDFT validation studies.

proposals.title: Inverse Solvent Design for Targeted Excited-State Conformers: A Quantum-Mechanical and Machine-Learning Strategy
proposals.motivation: Solvent environment critically influences molecular excited-state dynamics and photochemistry, but rationally designing solvents to stabilize a specific excited-state geometry remains a major challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Barone's on joint DFT/wave-function methods for conformational landscapes, provides accurate forward models of solvent effects on structure and spectroscopy, but the inverse problem is largely unaddressed. Solving it would enable the rational engineering of photochemical environments for applications in optoelectronics and photobiology.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting a machine-learned quantum-mechanical model that maps solvent descriptors to excited-state conformational energies and geometries, it is possible to computationally design solvent mixtures with properties that selectively stabilize a target excited-state molecular structure.
proposals.method: We will first build a high-accuracy forward model using Barone's established composite wave-function/DFT strategy (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS//DFT-D3) to compute excited-state (TD-DFT or NEVPT2) geometries and energies for a target molecule (e.g., a fluorescent amino acid derivative) in a diverse set of implicit and explicit solvent models. Key solvent properties (dielectric constant, polarity/polarizability, H-bond parameters) will be used as descriptors. A neural network or Gaussian Process will be trained to predict the excited-state conformational landscape from these descriptors. This model will then be inverted using a global optimization algorithm (e.g., Bayesian Optimization or a genetic algorithm) to search the solvent descriptor space for combinations that minimize the energy of the target excited-state conformer relative to others.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational framework for the inverse design of solvent environments, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide specific, testable solvent formulations (e.g., dielectric constant and H-bond donor/acceptor balance) to control excited-state structure. The methodology will directly extend the accuracy of quantum chemistry to the practical problem of solvent engineering.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of solvent effects on spectroscopy is established, the explicit inverse design of solvent properties to engineer a specific excited-state geometry is a non-obvious and unexplored application of machine-learning-enhanced quantum chemistry. It inverts the typical structure-spectroscopy relationship to one of spectroscopy/structure-guided solvent design.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Coarse-Graining for Large-System Anharmonic Calculations
ideas.description: This idea proposes to extend the automated anharmonic approach to larger molecular systems by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). In CFD, the intractable problem of simulating fluid flow across all relevant scales is solved using hierarchical multi-scale methods like Large Eddy Simulation (LES), where detailed simulation is reserved for critical small-scale turbulent regions, and coarser models handle the bulk flow. Adapted to computational spectroscopy, the solution is to implement a hierarchical coarse-graining scheme. The automated anharmonic methodology would be applied in full detail only to a pre-defined, chemically active 'core' region of a large, complex molecule (such as a chromophore in a protein or a reactive site in a catalyst). The remainder of the molecular system (the protein scaffold, solvent shell, or polymeric matrix) would be treated with progressively lower levels of theory—from reduced-dimensionality anharmonic models, to standard harmonic approximations, to efficient molecular mechanics force fields. The central technical challenge and innovation will be the development of automated, robust protocols to define these regions and to seamlessly interface the different theoretical descriptions, ensuring energy and property conservation across the boundaries. This approach directly targets the computational bottleneck, enabling the accurate calculation of anharmonic vibrational spectra for biologically or materially relevant large systems that are currently far beyond reach.

It computationally probes the influence of various coupling terms on predicted energy levels and selects only the most significant ones for inclusion in a refined, computationally efficient model. This ensures the resulting spectroscopic model is both more accurate than the rigid rotor harmonic oscillator approximation and tailored to the specific physics of the target molecule, avoiding unnecessary complexity. The workflow thus balances accuracy and computational cost based on quantifiable, automated criteria.

ideas.title: Implement State-Specific C-PCM for Robust Excited State Properties in Solution
ideas.description: This project develops a robust, linear-scaling solvation model for excited states by integrating a state-specific (SS) correction into the C-PCM framework. It first uses the efficient linear-response C-PCM to generate candidate excited states, then identifies potential charge-transfer excitations and recomputes their energies using a converged SS-PCM procedure for those states. This hybrid LR/SS approach maintains computational efficiency for local excitations while ensuring quantitative accuracy for charge-transfer processes. The resulting implementation provides a reliable, broadly applicable tool for predicting solvatochromic shifts, emission energies, and photochemical pathways.

In that field, materials and models are evaluated under extreme, non-linear loading to reveal weaknesses not seen in normal operation. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing that density functionals be benchmarked not on typical near-equilibrium properties, but by deliberately employing anharmonic potentials as a computational 'stress test.' An automated protocol would assess functionals' accuracy for vibrational spectra, bond dissociation, and other properties in highly anharmonic regimes, providing a dual-purpose benchmark: it scales to identify which functionals are robust for demanding applications and diagnoses specific failure modes related to electron correlation in non-quadratic potentials.

ideas.title: Semiclassical Lattice Dynamics for Anharmonic Spectra in Crystalline Solids
ideas.description: This research direction develops a computational framework that merges ab initio force constants for periodic solids with semiclassical initial value representation (SC-IVR) methods for quantum dynamics. The goal is to compute fully anharmonic infrared, Raman, and inelastic neutron scattering spectra directly from first principles, without relying on the perturbative limits of many-body phonon theory. This approach naturally captures multi-phonon processes, lifetime broadening, and the spectral consequences of strong electron-phonon coupling or structural instabilities. It thus provides a predictive, non-perturbative tool for interpreting and predicting complex spectroscopic features in materials ranging from thermoelectrics and superconductors to quantum paraelectrics.

ideas.title: Machine-Learned Collective Coordinates for Tractable Semiclassical Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the curse of dimensionality in semiclassical spectroscopy by learning a low-dimensional representation of the quantum dynamics. A neural network is trained on short-time quantum propagation data to discover a set of non-linear collective coordinates that encapsulate the system's key quantum behavior. The semiclassical initial value representation calculation is then performed in this learned coordinate space, where classical chaos is suppressed and trajectory proliferation is manageable. This enables accurate, non-perturbative spectral calculations for complex molecular and condensed-phase systems previously inaccessible to semiclassical methods.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Resource-Aware Workflow for Targeted High-Level Force Constant Refinement
ideas.description: This system automates the integration of high-accuracy ab initio methods for force constants in a computationally feasible manner. It employs a pilot calculation with a fast method to map the force constant landscape and identify the specific elements where high-level corrections are most critical for predictive accuracy. The workflow then performs targeted, high-level calculations only on these critical components, using techniques like machine-learned corrections or embedded quantum clusters to seamlessly blend accuracy levels. This results in a final force field that approaches the accuracy of a full high-level calculation at a fraction of the cost, making the method applicable to realistic materials and molecular systems.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Integration of Electronic Structure Methods via a Meta-Workflow
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes a hierarchical, intelligent integration of higher-level electronic structure methods into the automated workflow for force constant calculations, drawing an analogy from multi-fidelity modeling in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). In CFD, frameworks automatically manage transitions between computational models of varying cost and accuracy (e.g., from RANS to LES) based on localized solution requirements. Adapting this solution, we envision a meta-workflow controller for electronic structure that does not merely run a single high-level method, but strategically deploys a hierarchy of ab initio methods (from DFT to CCSD(T) or QMC). It would use cheaper methods for most force constant calculations but automatically identifies and triggers more accurate, expensive calculations for critical degrees of freedom, soft modes, or chemically complex regions where predictive accuracy is essential. This approach maximizes computational efficiency while providing the targeted accuracy of high-level methods where it matters most for the resulting vibrational properties.

ideas.title: Apply Graph Neural Networks for Force Field Fitting Inspired by Protein Folding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from the field of computational biology for protein structure prediction to the problem of force field fitting. The analogous problem—predicting a complex, multi-dimensional physical property (protein structure) from a compositional input (amino acid sequence)—was solved using graph neural networks trained on a large corpus of high-quality structural data. The adapted research idea is to represent a molecule as a spatial graph, with atoms as nodes and interatomic relationships as edges. A Graph Neural Network would then be trained on a vast dataset of electronic structure calculations (e.g., from DFT or CCSD(T)) to learn a direct mapping from the molecular graph to the complete set of anharmonic force constants. This approach inherently captures many-body and long-range interactions within the graph structure, enabling rapid, accurate, and transferable prediction of force fields for diverse molecules without requiring individual, costly quantum mechanical calculations for each new system of interest.

ideas.title: Predictive Resonance Network Mapping for Pre-emptive Hamiltonian Construction
ideas.description: This project develops a proactive computational framework that identifies and characterizes networks of strong anharmonic resonances prior to high-accuracy vibrational structure calculations. The core is an algorithm that analyzes low-level ab initio data—such as gradients, Hessians, and low-order force constants—to construct a graph of potentially coupled states based on energy gaps and coupling matrix element estimates. This predictive map is then used to guide the construction of tailored, adaptive basis sets or transformed Hamiltonians that intrinsically account for the resonance network, moving beyond post-hoc corrections to a first-principles, resilience-by-design approach for treating strongly coupled vibrational states.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dielectric Models from Seismic Wave Propagation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a static dielectric constant in optical and electronic property calculations by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, accurate modeling of wave propagation requires frequency-dependent velocity models to account for dispersion and attenuation. Adapting this solution, we propose developing first-principles or empirical models where the solvent's dielectric response is not a single number but a frequency-dependent function ε(ω). This adaptive dielectric model would be integrated into electronic structure calculations (e.g., TD-DFT, GW-BSE) to more accurately predict solvent effects on excitation energies, charge transfer states, and nonlinear optical properties, where the characteristic timescales of electronic processes interact with the solvent's intrinsic relaxation times.

ideas.title: Sparse Kernel Selection for Ultrafast Anharmonic Force Field Parameterization
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from compressed sensing in signal processing to the problem of creating minimal, ultrafast anharmonic force field models. The analogous problem—reconstructing a full signal from very few samples—is solved by exploiting signal sparsity via L1 regularization. Here, the 'signal' is the complete set of anharmonic force constants, which is assumed to be sparse (i.e., dominated by a few significant couplings, like those for specific bond stretches or key angle bends). The adapted solution involves running a limited set of targeted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations or applying specific displacement patterns to the system. These provide the non-uniform 'measurements.' A compressive sensing algorithm then solves for the force constants, using sparsity-promoting regularization to select only the most significant anharmonic terms. This results in a drastically downsized, physically interpretable model that captures essential anharmonicity for ultrafast simulation while bypassing the computational cost of a full parameterization.

ideas.title: Anharmonic Thermodynamics via Renormalization Group Flow
ideas.description: This idea applies the Renormalization Group (RG) methodology, a powerful technique from Quantum and Statistical Field Theory for handling strongly interacting systems across scales, to the problem of computing high-temperature thermodynamic properties where anharmonicity is critical. The analogous QFT solution involves integrating out high-energy degrees of freedom to derive an effective theory for low-energy physics, tracking how couplings flow with scale. Adapted to lattice dynamics, this becomes a procedure to iteratively integrate out high-frequency phonon modes, generating RG flow equations for the renormalization of anharmonic force constants with temperature. This scaled, effective Hamiltonian at each temperature scale then allows for the robust calculation of thermodynamic properties like free energy and specific heat in regimes where standard perturbative approaches to anharmonicity fail.

ideas.title: Develop a Universal Adapter Protocol for C-PCM Integration
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of coupling the C-PCM solvation model with a wider range of quantum chemical methods by drawing an analogy from bioinformatics. In that field, universal adapter protocols enable diverse molecular systems to interface with a single computational engine, like a docking scorer. The adapted solution is to develop a modular pre-processing 'adapter layer' for C-PCM. This layer would standardize the translation of key quantum mechanical data (electron densities, potentials, gradients) from any *ab initio* or DFT code into a common input format for the C-PCM solver. By creating specific adapter modules for different quantum chemistry packages (e.g., new DFT functionals, wavefunction-based methods), integration becomes a matter of connecting a new adapter, drastically broadening C-PCM's compatibility without rewriting its core algorithms.

ideas.title: Automated high-order force constant workflows via tensor factorization for ab initio methods
ideas.description: This research direction develops an automated computational workflow that integrates high-level electronic structure methods (e.g., CCSD(T), QMC) with tensor factorization techniques to manage anharmonic lattice dynamics. The workflow uses low-rank tensor approximations to compress the high-dimensional force constant data generated by these accurate but costly methods, drastically reducing storage and computational overhead. Crucially, the tensor decomposition identifies the dominant coupling patterns, which the workflow uses to intelligently select and prioritize subsequent ab initio calculations of force constants, creating a closed-loop, data-driven protocol. This synergy makes systematic studies of strong anharmonicity and high-temperature properties with quantum-mechanical accuracy computationally tractable for the first time.

ideas.title: Apply a surrogate model from computational fluid dynamics to predict solvation charges
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), where expensive turbulence model calculations are accelerated using neural network surrogate models. In CFD, a neural network is trained to predict the Reynolds stress tensor closure term, bypassing the iterative solution of complex turbulence equations during a simulation. We adapt this by training a neural network to act as a surrogate for the self-consistent boundary element calculation in implicit solvent models. Given inputs such as the solute's electrostatic potential on a discretized surface and the solvent's dielectric constant, the network would directly output the corresponding polarization surface charge distribution. This replaces the traditional iterative, matrix-solving procedure, accelerating the self-consistent cycle for molecular dynamics or energy calculations in solution.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Machine Learning for Generation of Physically Rigorous Anharmonic Force Fields
ideas.description: This approach employs machine learning models trained on electronic structure data to predict anharmonic force constants, but with a core redesign that guarantees physical robustness. The model architecture is fundamentally constrained to respect essential physical laws, such as rotational and translational invariance, and molecular point group symmetries, by operating within a symmetry-adapted coordinate framework. Instead of directly predicting force constants, the network learns a representation of the potential energy surface, ensuring that all predicted constants are internally consistent and derivable from a single, conservative potential. This results in force fields that are both rapidly generated and guaranteed to produce stable, physically meaningful dynamics for downstream simulations.

ideas.title: Refine Force Fields via Spectral Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the problem of refining imperfect physical models using sparse data from the field of Numerical Weather Prediction. In that field, data assimilation techniques like 4D-Var are used to nudge a forecast model's state and parameters to better match real-world observations. The analogous problem in computational chemistry is refining empirical force fields, which are often parameterized from limited data and struggle with anharmonic effects. The adapted solution is to frame force field refinement as a data assimilation problem: the computed anharmonic spectrum is the model forecast, and a target spectrum is the observation. A specialized assimilation algorithm is developed to inversely adjust force field parameters, minimizing the spectral mismatch. This creates a rigorous, automated, and scalable pipeline for deriving force fields that are quantitatively accurate for spectroscopic properties, directly addressing the inverse spectroscopic problem.

ideas.title: Adapting seismic wave propagation methods to semiclassical spectral computation
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from geophysical seismic imaging to semiclassical spectral computation. In seismology, Gaussian beam migration efficiently simulates wave propagation through complex geological structures by tracking bundles of rays with amplitude and phase information, providing accurate approximations beyond simple ray tracing while avoiding full-waveform computational costs. By transferring this approach to molecular systems, we can track semiclassical trajectory bundles through potential energy surfaces, where each bundle carries quantum amplitude and phase information analogous to seismic wavefronts. This enables computation of molecular spectra including quantum interference effects (like Franck-Condon progressions and recurrences) without solving the full Schrödinger equation, particularly valuable for large molecular systems or strong-field interactions where perturbative methods fail.

ideas.title: Sensitivity-Constrained Ultrafast Anharmonic Model Construction
ideas.description: This approach constructs a minimal, robust anharmonic model by automating the selection of force constants based on their quantified impact on target physical properties. A machine learning surrogate trained on a diverse dataset of atomic configurations performs a global sensitivity analysis to rank anharmonic couplings by importance. The model is then built iteratively, adding terms until predictions of key thermodynamic or kinetic properties converge, guaranteeing the model is both minimal and fit-for-purpose. This creates a transferable, uncertainty-aware parameterization method that avoids the pitfalls of ad-hoc truncation.

ideas.title: Active Learning of Anharmonic Free Energy Surfaces for High-Temperature Thermodynamics
ideas.description: This project develops a closed-loop computational framework to predict high-temperature thermodynamic properties in strongly anharmonic materials. It employs an active learning strategy where a machine-learned potential is iteratively trained and validated against quantum-accurate reference calculations, specifically targeting configurations sampled at elevated temperatures. The system directly computes anharmonic free energies via efficient thermodynamic integration from the learned potential, ensuring accuracy where classical potentials and perturbative methods fail. This approach provides a robust, scalable, and validated route to properties like heat capacity, thermal expansion, and phase stability in challenging high-T regimes.

ideas.title: Graph-Based Active Learning for Adaptive Solvation Force Fields
ideas.description: This research fuses graph-based cavity discretization with machine-learned force field fitting to create an adaptive framework for implicit solvation. A graph representation of the solute's solvent-accessible surface, where nodes are surface elements and edges encode geometric adjacency, is used to identify regions of high curvature or chemical complexity that are critical for accurate solvation energy predictions. A machine learning model, trained to predict local solvation contributions or cavity formation energies from electronic structure data, is then queried specifically for these graph-identified critical regions in an active learning loop. This synergy allows for the automated generation of optimally informative training data, leading to a solvation force field that dynamically improves its accuracy where it matters most, directly coupling the geometric representation of the solvent boundary with the data-driven parameterization of the underlying physics.

ideas.title: Anharmonicity-Aware Automated Phonon Framework for Crystalline Solids
ideas.description: This research system extends automated perturbative phonon calculations to periodic solids but incorporates a crucial diagnostic and adaptive layer. It first performs a preliminary screening to quantify the strength of anharmonic interactions using high-throughput finite-temperature simulations. For weakly anharmonic systems, it proceeds with the efficient automated perturbative approach. For materials exhibiting strong anharmonicity or incipient instabilities, it automatically deploys a robust, non-perturbative stochastic solver to compute accurate phonon properties. This hybrid, conditionally adaptive design ensures predictive reliability across the full spectrum of crystalline materials, from simple harmonic solids to strongly anharmonic and dynamically disordered phases.

ideas.description: This system automates the selection of parameterization methods by first predicting the performance and associated uncertainty for a given molecule across a portfolio of techniques. It embeds a decision hierarchy where low-uncertainty predictions allow efficient, direct calculation, while high-uncertainty cases trigger progressively more robust actions, such as running a small ensemble of methods or executing targeted high-level validation calculations. The core output is not just a set of parameters but a confidence-rated result with an audit trail of the decision logic, ensuring reliability on both common and novel molecular systems.

ideas.title: Adaptive Force Field Selection via Molecular Fingerprint Classification
ideas.description: This idea inverts the automation priority in molecular mechanics from brute-force calculation to intelligent, context-aware method selection, inspired by personalized medicine in oncology. In that field, automation focuses on diagnostic profiling (e.g., genetic sequencing) to classify a tumor and select a targeted therapy rather than applying a standard treatment to all patients. Adapted to computational chemistry, the research would develop an automated framework that first analyzes a molecule's structural and electronic 'fingerprint.' Based on this diagnostic classification (e.g., small organic, biomolecule, metal-organic framework), the system would then select and apply the most suitable force field or computational method from an available portfolio, optimizing for accuracy and efficiency per molecule rather than standardizing the calculation process.

ideas.title: Adaptive Algebraic Multigrid-Krylov Solver for Robust Implicit Solvation Calculations
ideas.description: This approach replaces a direct application of geometric multigrid with a robust, operator-adaptive solver hierarchy. It employs algebraic multigrid (AMG) to automatically construct coarse-grid corrections tailored to the specific numerical discretization of the solvation problem, effectively handling discontinuous dielectric coefficients. The system integrates this AMG preconditioner within a flexible outer Krylov subspace method (like FGMRES) to ensure convergence even for highly irregular problems. This results in a guaranteed and often optimal convergence rate for a wide range of molecular geometries and dielectric models, making the speed-up reliable and generalizable.
